content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 15, 2008

M. Lois Bobak
Woodruff, Spradlin & Smart
701 South Parker Street, Suite 8000
Orange, California 92868-4760

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-195

Dear Ms. Bobak:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Yorba Linda City
Councilmember Jan Horton regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We base this letter on the facts presented; the Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it
renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are
general in nature, and do not have enough information to make the necessary factual
determinations, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Also, please
note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no
opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law
conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

Questions

1. Does Ms. Horton have a conflict of interest that would prevent her from
participating in the city council's decision regarding the city's redevelopment plan?

2. Does she also have a conflict preventing her from participating in the city
council's decision regarding whether the city should hire a consultant to move the
redevelopment process forward?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-07-195
Page No. 2

3.     Can the city's Blue Ribbon Commission's (the "BRC") recommendations
be segmented from the rest of the decisions regarding the redevelopment plan allowing
Ms. Horton to participate in these decisions?

Conclusions

1.     Under the facts you provided in your previous letter (see Bobak Advice
Letter, I-07-141), and the supplemental facts you provided in our recent telephone
conversation, because Ms. Horton has property within 500 feet of one section of the
redevelopment area, she would be disqualified from any decision affecting the
redevelopment area as a whole and, if challenged, would have to rebut the presumption
that it is not reasonably foreseeable the decision will have any financial effect on her real
property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) Whether the recommendations of the BRC will
foreseeably and materially affect her property is a fact determination that we cannot
make.

2.     Ms. Horton may participate in the decision regarding whether the city will
hire a consultant and which consultant to hire.

3.     Yes. It appears that the BRC's recommendations can likely be segmented
from the decisions involving the rest of the redevelopment plan in some cases. We urge
you to apply the analysis found in our previous letter to determine whether segmenting
the decision is permissible.

Analysis

You are requesting follow-up advice on Bobak Advice Letter, I-07-041, of April,
2007. You spoke with us at length on January 9, 2008, and provided us with information
regarding the upcoming decisions facing the city council. The facts of your recent
request are basically the same as they were in your March 2007 letter, and we do not
recoun them here. The change that prompted you to write is that the Yorba Linda Blue
Ribbon Commission (the "BRC") has presented its findings to the city council. Whether
or not to accept the recommendations is now before the council. Your question is limited
to the issue of whether Ms. Horton may vote when the city council considers BRC's
recommendations and city staff's report on the next steps for the city's redevelopment
plan.

In our previous letter, we found that Ms. Horton has a conflict of interest due to
her economic interest in her personal residence, which is located within 500 feet of one
section of the development project area. We stated that if the city council can segment
decisions that would affect her property, she would be able to vote on some matters. At
the time, there was no particular decision before the city council and we based our
analysis on the information you provided.

Since that time, the BRC has offered several findings for the city council to
consider at its January 15, 2008 meeting. The recommendations are policy-based and
reflect the community's goals for the redevelopment area. Recommendations for the city
council include developing a plan for more open spaces and public facilities, encouraging
family-oriented entertainment, increasing flow for all modes of transportation, giving
incentives for restaurants and retail to move into the redevelopment area, and maintaining

File No. 1-07-195
Page No. 3

the existing character of the area. These are all broad recommendations that allow the
city council to understand what the public would like, and adopt some of the desires into
its thought-process for developing a specific plan.

From our view, the decisions do not fall into the types of decisions for
redevelopment plans in Regulation 18704.2(a)(5) as discussed in Step Four, pages 4-5 of
our April 2, 2007 letter. Thus, should the decisions specifically affect Site 13, which is
within 500 feet of Ms. Horton's personal residence, she may participate in the decision
and would have to provide proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable the decision will
have any financial effect on her real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).)

You have also provided us with the city staff's recommendations of what action
to take in moving forward. City staff recommends retaining a consultant to provide the
city with insight in developing a specific plan and undertaking feasibility studies
stemming from the BRC's recommendations. We have previously advised that even if a
public official has a conflict of interest on other decisions, she may participate in the
decision to choose a consultant with whom the city will work on the redevelopment plan.
(See Dean Advice Letter, A-94-059.)

When we spoke on the telephone, you stated that the city council's upcoming
meeting will present these initial decisions: the BRC's findings, the city staff's
recommendations to the city council. We strongly suggest that you apply the analysis
outlined in our April 2, 2007 letter to each decision as it comes before the council. If it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial affect on Site 13,
Ms. Horton would be disqualified. This analysis must be done on a decision-by-decision
basis.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl
